display.title: Meeting in the Upper Room
display.abstract: Centered on Belkis Ayón’s master print La cena (1991) in relation to Tomás Gutiérrez Alea’s film La última cena (1976), this chapter explores the ways Ayón’s practice, though deeply rooted in Cuban history and culture, serves as a searing critique of Cuba’s continuation of hegemonic racial and gender discrimination—despite the promises of revolution—through the brilliant redeployment of the founding myths of the all-male Abakuá society. The intergenerational discourse between Ayón and Alea’s work, demonstrates how contemporary art links and delinks with artistic forebears to extend criticality across time. It posits and proves that, aligned with Césaire’s decolonial vision, Ayón’s work encourages us to see how pivotal events like revolution and independence did not magically yield the egalitarian promises they are grounded on. Her art offers a chance to begin again by recognizing the need to decolonize histories and attendant values in an ongoing, generative process within the postcolonial.
